splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects how federal courts calculate sentencing guidelines at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, meaning the clock stops running and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term expires as stated in the judgment regardless of the supervisee's flight.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA and MPPAA require actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be established by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, as the date only fixes the data inputs, not the professional judgments applied to that data.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 authorizes claims for compensatory damages, or only injunctive relief, for discrimination in places of public accommodation.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the remedies available under Title II of the Civil Rights Act, with some circuits reading the statute to permit only injunctive relief and others allowing compensatory damages. This split affects the ability of individuals who experience discrimination at hotels, restaurants, and other public accommodations to obtain meaningful relief.

positions.label: Only injunctive relief available
positions.summary: Holds that Title II of the Civil Rights Act authorizes only injunctive relief, not compensatory damages, for discrimination in places of public accommodation.

positions.label: Compensatory damages available
positions.summary: Holds that Title II permits compensatory damages awards in addition to injunctive relief for discrimination in public accommodations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Courts are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or if possessing any one of the criteria is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentences for identically situated defendants depending on their circuit, implicating fundamental fairness in criminal sentencing.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines career-offender provision (U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b)) when the state criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance at issue to be federally scheduled. Some circuits hold that any state drug offense qualifies regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, while others require alignment with federal schedules. This affects career-offender designations and significantly impacts sentencing ranges.

positions.label: No federal-schedule limitation
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2(b) encompasses offenses under state law even if the particular substance is not controlled under federal law, finding no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Must match federal schedules
positions.summary: Holds that a state conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if it involves a substance also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid and authoritative interpretation of the guideline after Kisor v. Wilkie.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses deserves deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text itself does not mention inchoate offenses. This affects whether conspiracy convictions can trigger the career-offender enhancement, significantly increasing sentencing ranges.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid and controlling
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of the genuinely ambiguous guideline text and is entitled to deference, such that inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as career-offender predicates.

positions.label: Application Note 1 exceeds the guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition and is not entitled to deference after Kisor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law — specifically the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c) — may be considered as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law (such as the elimination of mandatory stacked § 924(c) sentences) as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others permit them as one factor among many. This affects thousands of federal prisoners serving lengthy sentences under pre-First Step Act stacking rules.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose punishment for supervised release violations, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the need to sanction the breach of trust and protect the public.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit has allowed district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations, while other circuits hold that revocation sentencing cannot be punitive in nature. This affects how courts frame and justify sentences for the hundreds of individuals who face revocation each year.

positions.label: Punishment for violations is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions, treating the revocation sentence as a sanction for the violating conduct.

positions.label: Revocation sentences should not be punitive
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of a revocation sentence is to sanction the breach of the court's trust and protect the public, not to punish the defendant for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the benefit of the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit holds it belongs to the estate, while other circuits and bankruptcy courts have reached the opposite conclusion. This split directly affects debtors' decisions about conversion and the distribution of assets to creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a) and the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor, not the estate, as § 348(f)(1)(A) limits the converted estate to property 'as of the date of filing' and was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal of a Hyde Amendment attorney's fees order in a criminal case is governed by the civil appeal deadline (60 days for government cases) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil or criminal matter for purposes of the applicable time limit to appeal. The majority view treats it as a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, applying the longer civil deadline, while at least one circuit has applied the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment order arises in a criminal case and thus the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause — which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker' — preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries arising from the transportation they arranged. This split affects the ability of injured parties to recover damages from brokers across the country.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted by the FAAAA because they relate to the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they are generally applicable common-law duties that do not specifically target broker services, routes, or prices.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence — claims that might also be raised under 28 U.S.C. § 2255 — as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for a sentence reduction under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release may serve as a vehicle for addressing potential legal errors in the original conviction or sentence, or whether such claims must be pursued exclusively through habeas corpus under § 2255. This affects prisoners who may have meritorious legal claims but face procedural barriers to § 2255 relief.

positions.label: May consider § 2255-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider legal errors or changes in law affecting the conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis under § 3582(c)(1)(A), even if such claims could also be raised under § 2255.

positions.label: May not use compassionate release for § 2255 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to circumvent the requirements of § 2255, and that claims challenging the legality of the conviction or sentence must be brought through habeas.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the FAA's Section 1 transportation-worker exemption — specifically how directly a worker's duties must involve the interstate movement of goods to qualify. This affects whether last-mile delivery drivers and similar distribution workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad exemption — distribution workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce, including last-mile delivery drivers, are transportation workers exempt from the FAA even if they do not personally cross state lines.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — distribution workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers or local distributors of goods are not transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce under Section 1 because they are not themselves part of the interstate transportation chain.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label beyond what the EPA required. This split affects products liability litigation against pesticide manufacturers nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing state juries to mandate different labeling requirements would impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims when they are consistent with (parallel to) FIFRA's requirements and do not require labels that conflict with EPA-approved labeling.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether state tort claims against a military contractor for injuries arising from combatant activities in a war zone are preempted by uniquely federal interests, even when the contractor violated its contractual obligations to the military.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the combatant-activities exception and federal preemption of state-law claims against military contractors. Some circuits hold that preemption applies broadly when injuries arise from combatant activities, while others limit preemption where the contractor deviated from its contractual obligations.

positions.label: Broad preemption — covers contractor deviations
positions.summary: Holds that state tort claims are preempted when injuries arise from combatant activities, even if the contractor allegedly violated contractual obligations, because allowing such suits would impermissibly second-guess military decisions.

positions.label: Narrow preemption — no preemption for contractual violations
positions.summary: Holds that preemption does not apply when the contractor violated its contractual obligations to the military, as such deviations are not authorized by the government and thus not shielded by the combatant-activities exception.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Harris v. Kellogg Brown & Root Services

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Commission has statutory authority to define non-retroactive changes in law as 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6), overriding circuit precedent to the contrary.
splits.description: Circuits are splitting on whether the Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to its policy statement — allowing prisoners to seek compassionate release based on unusually long sentences resulting from changes in law — is a binding exercise of delegated authority or exceeds the Commission's statutory mandate. This directly affects whether courts must follow the new policy statement or may adhere to prior circuit precedent categorically barring such claims.

positions.label: Commission exceeded its authority
positions.summary: Holds that the Sentencing Commission cannot override Congressional decisions about retroactivity by defining non-retroactive changes in law as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, and prior circuit precedent controls.

positions.label: Commission acted within its authority
positions.summary: Holds that the Sentencing Commission has delegated authority under 28 U.S.C. § 994(t) to define extraordinary and compelling reasons, and the new policy statement is binding on courts.
